title: Toward Heisenberg-Limited Interferometry with Dual Squeezers
authors: Yi Gu, Song-Ping Wang, Wei Zhong
abstract: The canonical Mach-Zehnder interferometer fed with a coherent state and a squeezed-vacuum state of equal intensities is theoretically predicted to achieve Heisenberg scaling in phase sensitivity. However, this ultimate performance is unattainable using direct photon-number-difference detection due to a divergence arising precisely at the optimal equal-intensity regime. In this work, we introduce a dual-squeezing approach that overcomes this fundamental limitation. Our scheme employs an additional single-mode squeezer before detection, forming a paired configuration with the input squeezer used to generate the squeezed-vacuum state. We analytically demonstrate that the resulting dual-squeezing Mach-Zehnder interferometer enables Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity with di rect photon-number-difference detection, while remaining robust against detection noise. Our work provides a feasible and robust route toward quantum-limited interferometric phase measurements
summary: The paper proposes a new dual-squeezing interferometer design that overcomes the fundamental limitation where phase sensitivity diverges in standard setups. By adding a second squeezer at the output, the scheme achieves Heisenberg-limited sensitivity that remains robust even with imperfect detectors. This provides a feasible path toward high-precision quantum-limited measurements.
main_problem: Standard Mach-Zehnder interferometers (MZI) fail to achieve Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity using direct photon-number-difference detection in the optimal equal-intensity regime due to a vanishing signal and divergence in sensitivity.
main_result: The introduction of a dual-squeezing approach (DS-MZI) eliminates the signal divergence and enables Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity that is robust against detection noise and photon loss.
method: The authors use an analytical framework involving SU(2) and SU(1,1) algebraic operators, error propagation formulas, and Wigner function analysis to evaluate phase sensitivity and robustness.
model_or_system: A Dual-Squeezing Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (DS-MZI) consisting of two single-mode squeezers (one at the input and one at the output), beam splitters, and a phase shifter, fed with a coherent state and a squeezed-vacuum state.
key_observables: Photon-number-difference (N-), total photon-number (N+), and phase sensitivity (delta phi).
important_parameters: Squeezing parameters (r1, r2), detection efficiency (eta), input amplitude (alpha), and the equal-intensity regime (alpha^2 approx sinh^2 r).
main_assumptions: The interferometer is assumed to be lossless for the derivation of the Quantum Fisher Information, and the optical elements are assumed to preserve Gaussian statistics.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the Wigner function distributions showing the elliptical distribution of the DS-MZI; Figure 3 compares the scaled phase sensitivity of the conventional MZI versus the DS-MZI under imperfect detection efficiency.
paper_structure: The paper identifies the divergence problem in standard MZI, introduces the DS-MZI architecture, provides an analytical derivation of phase sensitivity using operator algebra, evaluates performance under imperfect detection, and compares the results to the Heisenberg limit.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it proposes a practical, robust method to reach the fundamental Heisenberg limit in interferometry, overcoming the practical limitations of signal vanishing and detection loss.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(Color online) Schematic of (a) the conventional Caves scheme using a standard Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI) fed by a coherent state (CS) and a squeezed-vacuum state (SVS), and (b) the DS-MZI scheme, where a vacuum state (VS) is squeezed by S1 before entering the MZI, and an additional squeezer S2 is applied to one output mode prior to detection. The Wigner quasi-probability distributions for the output ports are plotted for equal input intensities.
